attributes.stem:
An 81-year-old man presents with a six day history of pain and redness on his left leg (Image). He feels generally unwell and feverish. There has not been any obvious trauma to his leg. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes and hypertension for which he takes metformin, sitagliptin and captopril.

On examination his blood pressure is 140/89 mmHg, pulse rate 90/min and temperature 38.3˚C. His BMI is 20. There is a tender, warm, erythematous area with poorly demarcated margins covering most of his left leg.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This presentation is consistent with cellulitis. This is an acute bacterial infection causing inflammation of the deep dermis and surrounding subcutaneous tissue, leading to erythematous changes with poorly demarcated margins. Cellulitis is most frequently caused by group A streptococcus (i.e., Streptococcus pyogenes). The patient can present with a history of trauma, recent surgery or an underlying skin disorder. There may be co-morbid conditions that increase the risk for cellulitis including an immunocompromised state (either due to underlying disease or medications), for example, diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease and chronic liver disease.

Erysipelas is a bacterial skin infection caused by Streptococcus pyogenes involving the upper dermis typically on the face or legs, that characteristically extends into the superficial cutaneous lymphatics. It is a tender, intensely erythematous, indurated plaque with raised and sharply demarcated borders that help differentiate it from cellulitis. Patients often cannot recall an inciting event, but a history of recent trauma or pharyngitis may be elicited. Prodromal symptoms, such as malaise, chills, and high fever, often begin before the onset of the skin lesions.

Necrotising fasciitis, also known as 'flesh-eating disease', is a rapidly progressive inflammatory infection of the fascia, with secondary necrosis of the subcutaneous tissues. The causative bacteria may be aerobic, anaerobic, or mixed flora. A history of trauma, recent surgery or even a minor insect bite are common causative events. Immunocompromised condition, advancing age, obesity, diabetes, drug abuse, malignancies and chronic systemic diseases are common risk factors. The patient presents with severe progressive pain and the affected area starts to swell and shows a purplish rash which then turns into blisters filled with dark fluid. The wound then become necrotic and may exhibit a characteristic fine crackling sensation under the skin (crepitus) due to gas in the tissues.

Increased pressure in the veins of the legs is called chronic venous hypertension. Chronic venous hypertension may be due to venous insufficiency or obstruction in the veins because of obesity, previous venous thrombosis, or compression of abdominal or pelvic veins. Symptoms of chronic venous hypertension may include swelling, pitting oedema, brown-coloured skin (haemosiderin deposition), particularly near the ankles, tenderness, tight feeling calves or itchy painful legs, venous stasis or venous leg ulcers. The changes seen with gross haemosiderin deposition can sometimes resemble the appearance of cellulitis.

Wells syndrome (eosinophilic cellulitis) is an uncommon recurrent inflammatory skin disease of unknown aetiology. The presentation usually involves a mildly pruritic or tender cellulitis-like eruption which is cool to touch, differentiating it from bacterial cellulitis. The clinical presentation may also include annular plaques, vesicles and bullae. It is a hypersensitivity reaction, systemic symptoms, including asthma, arthralgia, and fever, may be evident, although they usually do not occur.
learningPoints: Cellulitis is an acute bacterial infection causing inflammation of the deep dermis and surrounding subcutaneous tissue causing an erythematous lesion with poorly demarcated margins.
references: Gabillot-Carré M, Roujeau JC. Acute bacterial skin infections and cellulitis. Curr Opin Infect Dis. 2007 Apr. 20(2):118-23.
McNamara DR, Tleyjeh IM, Berbari EF, et al. Incidence of lower-extremity cellulitis: a population-based study in Olmsted county, Minnesota. Mayo Clin Proc. Jul 2007. 82(7):817-21.
